title: VCDA Outage Due to vCD LDAP Authentication Failure
problem: vCloud Director Availability (VCDA) experienced an outage
root_cause: VCDA depends on vCloud Director (vCD) for authentication and service operations vCD failed to authenticate due to an LDAP connectivity failure caused by SSL (LDAPS) enforcement
resolution: Resolved by correcting vCD LDAP authentication to use LDAPS

title: VM Provisioning Failure Due to Broken vcenter-HPCS Trust
problem: VM provisioning was failing
root_cause: vcenter could not coordinate with IBM Cloud Hyper Protect Crypto Services (HPCS) due to a broken trust relationship Variations in vSphere/vcenter versions, HPCS versions, and encryption configurations caused HPCS to present different public and private server certificates, leading to certificate validation failures and loss of trust
resolution: Resolved by trusting the HPCS server certificates

title: VCDA Replication Failures in Red State Post-vcenter Upgrade
problem: vCloud Director Availability (VCDA) experienced replication failures in a Red state following a vcenter upgrade
root_cause: Replication tracking (RT) Virtual machines (VMs) were marked invalid after being detected in an Unknown state This state transition was caused by a residual vcenter reconnect call present in the SDDC upgrade and patching code—an impact documented in Broadcom Knowledge base (KB) KB410559 Although preventive actions were previously identified to remove all such reconnect references, one instance remained unnoticed, resulting in the recurrence of the issue
resolution: Identified and removed the remaining vcenter reconnect call reference from SDDC upgrade code

title: VCFaaS Linux VMs within Cluster have lost connectivity to their discs
problem: Some virtual machines became unavailable during host maintenance activities in the VCFaaS environment
root_cause: A defect in the VCFaaS Host Add automation resulted in vsan entering an unstable state when a host was placed into maintenance mode without selecting the “Ensure Accessibility” option Without this option, required data components were not evacuated, leading to temporary VM unavailability
resolution: The impacted virtual machines were recovered by restarting them. As a corrective measure, host maintenance procedures were reviewed to ensure the “Ensure Accessibility” option is used during short-term maintenance, preventing recurrence

title: Veeam repository VMs became unavailable
problem: The vsan cluster running on Cascade Lake (CXL) hosts experienced performance degradation and intermittent I/O errors when compared to the Sapphire Rapids (SPR) cluster
root_cause: When new hosts were added to the Cascade Lake vsan cluster, automatic data rebalancing was triggered, leading to vsan resynchronization activity. Due to the limited performance and scalability of the Cascade Lake hardware, the cluster was unable to handle the additional resync workload effectively, resulting in performance issues and I/O errors
resolution: The cluster was upgraded to Sapphire Rapids (SPR) hosts, and sufficient compute and storage resources were added. This improved overall cluster performance, allowed vsan resynchronization to complete efficiently, and eliminated the observed I/O errors

title: VM backups impacted
problem: Backup operations were disrupted due to loss of communication between Veeam Backup & Replication (VBR) and its SQL database
root_cause: A network-related issue impaired the connection to the SQL Server, impacting communication between the VBR application and the database
resolution: The VBR server was rebooted, which restored database connectivity and allowed backup operations to resume successfully

title: nsx T hosts were degraded
problem: Several virtual machines entered an inaccessible state due to network connectivity issues within the nsx environment
root_cause: nsx Transport hosts became degraded, resulting in GRE tunnel failures. As a result, packets were not exchanged between the tunnel endpoints, leading to loss of network connectivity for the affected virtual machines
resolution: The degraded hosts were placed into maintenance mode and removed from service for further investigation by the hardware team. The impacted virtual machines were migrated to healthy hosts, restoring normal operations

title: VDC Settings option is not available for the tenants
problem: The Edit option was not visible in the tenant UI of vCloud Director (vCD)
root_cause: The February go-live code change inadvertently removed the vCD: Edit permission from the default rights bundle, which resulted in the option not being displayed in the tenant UI
resolution: The code was corrected to restore the vCD: Edit permission in the default rights bundle, resolving the UI visibility issue
Change: VCFaaS Feb 2025 release bringing new features, updates, issue fixes, and security enhancements to production

title: Veeam Backup Portal UI Inaccessible
problem: The integration between vCloud Director (Director) and Veeam Enterprise Manager (EM) in the FRA environment was not functioning
root_cause: An apparent UI bug in Director led to the Veeam EM URL not being updated during configuration. This was a human error, where the operator did not update the required Veeam EM URL in the Director UI, resulting in the integration being inadvertently broken
resolution: The Veeam Enterprise Manager configuration in Director was updated with the correct URL, which restored the Director–Veeam EM integration.
impact: Customers unable to reach the Veeam Backup Portal UI

title: Windows activation failing for newly provisioned VDCs
problem: Customers were unable to activate Windows virtual machines, resulting in failed activation transactions for newly provisioned VDCs.
root_cause: The February 2025 VCFaaS automation update introduced a default deny-all firewall configuration for newly provisioned VDCs as a security enhancement. This removed previously allowed outbound access to IBM Cloud private services, causing Windows OS activation failures until customers explicitly enabled outbound firewall rules to the required endpoints.
resolution: Customers were guided to manually configure the required NAT and firewall rules to allow outbound connectivity to Windows activation endpoints (including TCP 1688). Public documentation was updated via PTASK0070620 to clearly state customer responsibility for configuring firewall and NAT rules.

title: VCDA performance degradation causing VM migration issues
problem: Customers experienced socket errors, slow replication, and multiple virtual machines failing to power on after VCDA cutover.
root_cause: The issue was triggered by a configuration change related to vcenter High Availability (VCHA), where an added DNS entry on the management vcenter disrupted connectivity between vcenter and the VCDA service. This dependency exists in Gen1 VCDA architecture, where VCDA manager and tunnel rely on the management vcenter. Concurrent cluster resource constraints further amplified the impact, resulting in VM power-on failures and degraded performance.
resolution: vcenter was re-paired with VMware Cloud Director to restore VCDA connectivity. Cluster CPU and memory reservations were adjusted, and workloads were rebalanced. Documentation and runbooks were updated to capture the Gen1 VCDA dependency on management vcenter and to better plan VCHA-related changes.
impact: VM Migration Failure and Performance Degradation
Change: Configure vcenter High Availability (VCHA) on vcenter

title: Network connectivity disruption during nsx Edge maintenance
problem: Customers experienced loss of network connectivity and GRE tunnel traffic interruption during planned maintenance activity on nsx Edges.
root_cause: During a maintenance action to resize an nsx Edge for mitigating network slowness, an existing nsx misconfiguration related to the BGP Graceful Restart and Helper setting caused an extended failover duration. When the active Edge was taken offline, traffic failed over to the standby Edge, but due to the misconfigured Graceful Restart setting with third-party BGP peers, failover took approximately 6.5 minutes instead of the expected ~30 seconds, resulting in customer impact.
resolution: Engineering mitigated the impact by deploying new Edge nodes and restoring network connectivity. nsx Edge maintenance activities were paused, and follow-up tasks were created to correct the Graceful Restart and Helper configuration and align future maintenance procedures with known disruption behavior.
Change: Replacement of small form factor edge with x-large form factor edge

title: Veeam repository connection failures impacting backup operations
problem: Customers experienced failures connecting to the Veeam Backup and Recovery (VBR) repository, resulting in backup and restore job failures.
root_cause: The VBR server experienced sustained high CPU and memory utilization, which caused PostgreSQL database connectivity failures and prevented access to the Veeam console. The exact underlying trigger for the resource exhaustion could not be conclusively identified at the time. Uptycs activity, including YARA scanning and memory utilization, was investigated as a potential contributing factor, but no definitive causal link was confirmed.
resolution: The incident was mitigated by restarting the Veeam Backup and Recovery service, which restored database connectivity and allowed backup jobs to resume. Logs were collected and shared with Veeam for further analysis, and follow-up tasks were created to review resource sizing, monitoring, and Uptycs behavior to prevent recurrence.

title: Datastore capacity exhaustion causing backup and VM operation failures
problem: Backup jobs failed and customers experienced VM power-on failures and pending VM states due to datastore capacity exhaustion.
root_cause: During customer-initiated workload migrations, all VCDA replications were placed on a single NFS datastore, exhausting its capacity, even though another datastore with significant free space was available. The VCDA relies on VMware Cloud Director for datastore placement, and in this scenario vCD repeatedly returned the same datastore, leading to imbalance. Additionally, alerting gaps occurred because the Slack webhook depended on a decommissioned DNS setup, preventing timely notification when the datastore reached full capacity.
resolution: The issue was mitigated by obtaining customer approval to delete and recreate replications, freeing over 670 GB of space on the impacted datastore. Additional NFS storage capacity was provisioned, and backup operations were validated as back to business as usual. Follow-up problem tasks were opened to review VCDA placement strategy with Broadcom and to fix alerting and notification gaps.
impact: Backup Job Failures and VM Power-On Failures

title: nsx Manager UI inaccessible affecting new workload provisioning
problem: Customers were unable to provision new VSIs because the nsx Manager UI was inaccessible, resulting in missing organization networks during new workload creation.
root_cause: A known nsx software defect caused the Corfu compactor leader to become unresponsive due to the absence of leader rotation over time. This prevented compaction cycles from running, leading to excessive memory consumption by the Cluster Boot Manager (CBM) service. As memory was exhausted, the CBM service crashed, rendering the nsx Manager UI inaccessible.
resolution: Infra engineers rebooted all nsx Manager controller nodes to restore the Corfu compactor and CBM services, which brought the nsx UI back to a healthy state. New provisioning was validated successfully post-recovery. A longer-term mitigation plan was created to prevent recurrence until upgrade to nsx version 4.2.2.
impact: New VSI and network provisioning failures

title: Partially deleted Edge caused metering and billing interruption
problem: Metering and billing processes stopped after an Edge associated with a Virtual Data Center (VDC) was left in a partially deleted state, blocking interval job completion.
root_cause: A cleanup script intended to remove orphaned Tier-0 (T0) networking components was mistakenly executed on a VDC that was not approved for deletion. Because the VDC still had a Tier-1 (T1) network attached, the script only partially deleted the networking configuration, leaving the Edge in a broken state.
resolution: Engineers manually deleted the remaining T1 network from nsx and restarted the stalled deletion task. Once corrected, pending metering interval jobs resumed and completed successfully.
impact: Billing and metering interruption for multi-tenant environments
Change: Manually clean up broken VRF T0 instances using the provided script

title: VM provisioning and migration failures with encrypted vsan storage policies
problem: Customers were unable to order or migrate virtual machines using encrypted vsan storage policies.
root_cause: A network configuration change unintentionally introduced a duplicate subnet on an SRX device, causing incorrect routing and intermittent connectivity loss between ESXi hosts and vcenter.
resolution: Engineers powered off the misconfigured SRX, corrected routing, restarted affected ESXi hosts, and resynchronized storage providers in vcenter.
impact: VM provisioning and migration failures with encrypted storage
Change: Adding additional capacity to DALPR to meet customer demand

title: VCDA replications entered invalid state after vcenter certificate rotation
problem: Customer VM replications using VMware Cloud Director Availability failed, showing an invalid state.
root_cause: Operators followed an incomplete runbook during vcenter SSL certificate rotation, failing to accept the new certificate on VCDA appliances.
resolution: Engineers accepted the new certificate on all VCDA appliances and reconfigured affected replications.
Change: Renewal of the certificates to prevent access issues

title: Firewall logs unavailable in vCloud Director Edge Gateway
problem: Customers were unable to view or export firewall logs under the Edge Gateway section in vCloud Director.
root_cause: A service account used for vCD–vRLI integration was disabled, and required reconfiguration for existing environments was not performed.
resolution: Engineers reconfigured the integration with a new automation service account and restored log ingestion.
impact: Firewall log visibility and export unavailable

title: Backup Job Failures due to Internal Connectivity Issue
problem: Backup jobs may fail or not complete successfully
root_cause: nsx Manager was stuck in a reconfiguring state while updating static routes associated with the AVI VIP. This prevented internal components such as VBR servers from reaching the vCloud Director (vCD) VIP due to a known nsx issue, resulting in backup job failures.
resolution: Restarted nsx Manager nodes sequentially to force re-synchronization of static routes with ESXi routing tables, restoring connectivity and backup operations
impact: Backup job failures and delays; internal service access impacted while external customer access remained unaffected

title: VM Connectivity Loss due to nsx Manager Communication Failure
problem: Virtual machines became unreachable due to network connectivity loss
root_cause: A known JDK defect caused nsx managers to reach thread limits, preventing communication with edge nodes. This stopped propagation of tunnel configuration, resulting in GENEVE tunnel failure. The issue was triggered during an ongoing nsx upgrade.
resolution: Performed rolling restart of nsx Manager nodes to restore communication with edge nodes and re-establish tunnels, recovering connectivity
impact: Approximately 2000 VMs became inaccessible and multiple applications lost connectivity

title: Veeam Backup Failure due to VBR Database Resource Bottleneck
problem: Backup jobs were failing and not progressing
root_cause: VBR database had insufficient resources after VBR scaling. Concurrent execution of multiple full backup jobs along with vsan resync caused high IO latency and resource exhaustion, and the database was not resized accordingly
resolution: Allowed vsan resync to complete to stabilize storage performance, restarted failed backup jobs, balanced workload across multiple proxies to avoid saturation, temporarily disabled Uptycs to reduce database interference, increased VBR database resources to handle workload demand, and optimized backup job scheduling to prevent simultaneous resource spikes
impact: Users were unable to create or run backup jobs, with multiple jobs failing or experiencing significant delays

title: nsx Edge Provisioning Failure due to Controller Overload and Message Storm
problem: New edge nodes provisioning was failing and nodes were stuck in not ready state
root_cause: Existing edge nodes generated excessive synchronization traffic due to a known nsx issue, which overloaded and destabilized the nsx controllers. This prevented timely processing of new edge node messages, resulting in failed configuration and registration
resolution: Reduced load on nsx controllers by deleting and powering off unnecessary edge nodes, performed controlled rolling restarts of nsx controllers and managers to clear message queues, monitored and analyzed message queue buildup using vendor-provided scripts, applied workarounds to stabilize controller communication, engaged vendor for root cause analysis and hotfix, and implemented actions to minimize message storm and improve system stability
impact: Provisioning of new edge nodes failed, leading to delays in network setup while existing active nodes continued functioning

title: Veeam Backup Jobs Stuck due to Uptycs Interference with VBR
problem: Backup jobs were stuck and not progressing
root_cause: Uptycs was missing exclusion tags for VBR, causing real-time scanning of VBR processes. This interfered with read-write operations and made the VBR system unresponsive
resolution: Restarted the VBR server to restore responsiveness and resume backup jobs, temporarily reduced interference by addressing Uptycs behavior, validated and corrected exclusion tagging for VBR processes, ensured proper handling of database connections and system resources, implemented monitoring checks for tagging consistency, and improved operational steps to identify and mitigate similar performance degradation scenarios
impact: Backup jobs were delayed or stuck, leading to missed backup schedules and operational impact

title: VDC Provisioning Failures with Edge Gateway due to vCD API and Platform Version Incompatibility
problem: VDC provisioning requests with Edge Gateway selection were failing or getting stuck in a non-completed state, causing delays and failed transactions for customers
root_cause: A version mismatch between the vCloud Director APIs and the production vCloud Director platform caused API communication failures. The API version was upgraded to v39.1 and validated in staging with vCD version 10.6.1, but the production environment was not upgraded to v10.6.1, making it incompatible with the newer API version. This mismatch prevented successful execution of provisioning API calls
resolution: Reverted the API version used by the microservice to a compatible version and deployed the fix through CI/CD pipelines. Refreshed service pods and restarted failed provisioning tasks to ensure successful completion. Additionally, corrective actions were defined including enforcing version compatibility checks, aligning staging and production environments, and enhancing pre-release validation and testing processes
impact: Customers experienced failures or delays while provisioning VDCs with Edge Gateway enabled, while provisioning without Edge Gateway and existing workloads remained unaffected
Change: VCFaaS Sept 2025 release bringing new features, updates, issue fixes, and security enhancements to production

title: Backup Job Failures due to Service Account Lockout from Incorrect Credential Usage
problem: Multiple backup jobs were failing due to authentication errors, causing unsuccessful backup operations
root_cause: The Veeam VBR service account was locked because Veeam One, using the same service account, repeatedly attempted authentication with an incorrect password. This continuous use of wrong credentials led to account lockout, preventing successful authentication and execution of backup jobs. The exact reason for Veeam One using an incorrect password remains under investigation with the vendor
resolution: The issue was mitigated by stopping Veeam One to prevent further account lockouts and restoring backup job operations. A new Veeam One VM was created, connected to the database to validate and update credentials, and then failed back to the original system. Passwords were updated across components using vendor-recommended procedures, restoring normal operations. Follow-up actions include vendor investigation, improving credential management, and implementing safeguards to prevent service account lockouts
impact: Customers experienced failures in backup job execution, leading to unsuccessful backup operations until the issue was resolved

title: nsx Certificate Auto-Renewal Causing Authentication Failure between vCD and nsx
problem: Users encountered errors when accessing firewall rules due to inability to communicate with nsx services
root_cause: An nsx feature introduced in version 4.2.1 automatically renewed appliance certificates before expiry. The new certificate was not trusted by vCloud Director because there was no mechanism to synchronize or update the certificate in vCD. This caused authentication failure between vCD and nsx, breaking communication
resolution: The issue was resolved by manually trusting the newly renewed nsx certificate in vCloud Director to restore communication. Follow-up actions include evaluating whether to enable or disable the auto-renewal feature, aligning certificate management processes, and coordinating with vendor guidance for handling certificate updates
impact: Users were unable to access nsx-backed services such as firewall, NAT, and other edge gateway functionalities, leading to service disruption

title: VM Management Failures during ESXi Upgrade due to Forced vcenter and vCD Synchronization
problem: Customers experienced intermittent failures while performing virtual machine management actions such as power operations and snapshots during an upgrade
root_cause: During the ESXi upgrade process, a forced synchronization between vcenter and vCloud Director was triggered to align hardware versions. This involved repeated reconnections of the VCD API to vcenter, which temporarily disrupted API operations and caused VM management actions to fail. The impact was not identified earlier due to lack of test validation for such scenarios during SVT
resolution: The issue was mitigated by removing a problematic ESXi host from the cluster and restarting the upgrade tasks, allowing the upgrade process to proceed successfully. Additional corrective actions include enhancing SVT coverage to include VM operations during upgrades, modifying upgrade workflows to move force synchronization to the end, updating runbooks to avoid restarting specific tasks, and improving validation of automation steps during upgrades
impact: Customers experienced intermittent failures in virtual machine management actions during upgrade windows, though normal operations resumed after mitigation
Change: Remediate 9.3 CVE vulnerability (VMSA-2025-0013) on all ESXi hosts

title: VDC Provisioning Failure due to Incorrect T1-T0 Validation Logic in Network Code
problem: VDC creation requests were failing, blocking provisioning activities across multiple regions
root_cause: A software defect in the VDC creation logic caused the system to fetch and validate all existing T1 network instances instead of only those related to the target VDC. If any unrelated T1 instance was not connected to a T0, the validation failed, resulting in provisioning failure
resolution: The issue was mitigated by deploying a code fix that bypassed validation for unrelated T1 instances and restored provisioning functionality. Additional corrective actions were defined, including validating only VDC-specific T1 instances, improving network mapping logic, implementing monitoring for T1-T0 connectivity issues, and enhancing automation to detect and handle dangling T1 instances
impact: Customers across multiple regions experienced failures in VDC provisioning due to T1 network creation issues, leading to service delays

title: Connectivity Issue between VCFaaS and PowerVS
problem: Customer connectivity from VCFaaS to Power VSI via the Transit Gateway failed due to BGP session outages, which were caused by an unintended deletion of a transit gateway subnet triggered by a defect in an automated cleanup script.
root_cause: The root cause of the issue was a defect introduced in a cleanup script modification aimed at standardizing nsx API behavior across environments. This defect caused identical values to be assigned to both subnets meant for preservation and deletion, resulting in the unintended removal of a subnet actively used by the Transit Gateway. Consequently, connectivity between the Transit Gateway and its resources was disrupted, leading to BGP session failures and loss of connectivity between VCFaaS and Power VSI. The defect went undetected due to inadequate adherence to established testing protocols.
resolution: Identified and restored secondary classic subnet
Change: VCFaaS Nov 2025 release bringing new features, updates, issue fixes, and security enhancements to production

title: Edge Deployments are failing in Dallas and its affecting customer
problem: Edge deployment failed due to an expired nsx Manager certificate preventing OVF signature validation.
root_cause: The edge deployment failure in the DAL environment was caused by an expired internal certificate (expired on January 3, 2026) used by the nsx Manager to sign the edge OVF template. This prevented the nsx Manager from validating OVF signatures, halting the deployment process. The certificate expiration is a known issue affecting multiple nsx versions, as documented by Broadcom.
resolution: Identified and applied fix recommended by Broadcom in KB424034

title: VPN Tunnel Between FortiGate and VMware Cloud Director (DR) Not Establishing
problem: VPN tunnel between FortiGate and VMware Cloud Director (DR) is not establishing, resulting in no connectivity between endpoints.
root_cause: The VPN tunnel failed to establish due to lack of return traffic from the T1 gateway (VMware Cloud Director side). Although outbound traffic was successfully sent from T0, no response was received from T1, indicating a likely issue within the nsx (Broadcom-managed) network that requires further investigation.
resolution: The customer changed the peer IP and the VPN was restored, It appeard to be a customer issue since particular IP being blocked

title: VLP customers are facing intermittent connectivity issue
problem: Customers experienced intermittent connectivity issues on the VLP
root_cause: The issue was caused by an inappropriately low timeout value (30 seconds) configured in the nsx load balancer application profile, which led to connections being prematurely terminated during idle periods.
resolution: Updated keep-Alive timeout from 30seconds to 5minutes on VCFaaS AVI Load balancer

title: Customer tasks are failing in vCD
problem: Customer operations on vCloud Director were failing due to an authentication breakdown with vcenter, impacting normal platform functionality.
root_cause: Customer task failures on vCloud Director occurred due to a disruption in the vcenter integration, caused by authentication failure. This happened because vCloud Director was misconfigured to use an administrator account instead of the designated service account, and the administrator account password was changed externally. The origin of this misconfiguration could not be determined, as no evidence was found in automation, logs, or incident records.
resolution: Incorrect vcenter credentials were identified and updated to the designated service account. The vcenter connection was reconfigured, reconnected, and refreshed along with storage policies, restoring normal vCloud Director operations.
impact: Service Disruption / Authentication Failure, Cloud Management Platform (vCD Operations)

title: 15 VMs backup failed with other errors
problem: Backup jobs for 15 virtual machines failed because snapshot operations on the ESXi host were not functioning. This issue occurred due to the hostd service becoming unresponsive, preventing successful backup execution.
root_cause: Backup failures occurred because the ESXi host’s hostd service became unresponsive, which disrupted snapshot operations required for backups. The underlying cause was a hardware failure (faulty DIMM memory) that led to the hostd service hang.
resolution: The issue was resolved by replacing the faulty DIMM memory identified by SoftLayer, restoring hostd service stability and normal snapshot/backup operations.

title: Backup got failed for 7vm's in BNPP W004
problem: Backup failures occurred for 7 VMs due to an ESXi host becoming unresponsive, preventing proper snapshot and backup operations.
root_cause: The backup failures for the 7 VMs were caused by an ESXi host becoming unresponsive due to a ESXi boot bank communication issue between the VMkernel and the boot device. This prevented proper snapshot and backup handling. The issue was likely triggered by a temporary software/firmware glitch rather than a hardware fault, as normal functionality was restored after a host reboot.
resolution: The issue was resolved by obtaining approval and performing a reboot of the affected ESXi host (w004p04esx012), which restored communication with the ESXi boot device and brought the host back to a responsive state. Post-reboot, backup operations were monitored and confirmed to be functioning normally.

title: VCDA test and failover actions are failing with unexpected error
problem: VCDA test and failover operations are failing due to incorrect datastore inaccessibility detection, despite the datastore being healthy and operational.
root_cause: VCDA test and failover operations failed because the datastore was reported as inaccessible by VCDA, despite being healthy and supporting ongoing replications; the exact cause of this incorrect accessibility detection is under investigation with Broadcom (case #36828414).
resolution: Refreshed backend datastore, which resolve the issue

title: Customer VM was inaccessible in FRA
problem: A VM in FRA became inaccessible due to an ESXi host outage caused by an out-of-memory condition, resulting in I/O errors and file locks preventing failover.
root_cause: The VM became inaccessible because an ESXi host (w002f04esx021) entered an out-of-memory condition caused by a long-running packet capture, leading to I/O failures and file locks that prevented another host from acquiring ownership of the VM.
resolution: The affected host was placed in maintenance mode and rebooted to clear the issue causing VM file locks. This restored access to the impacted VM. Most VMs had already recovered via restart, and the remaining inaccessible VM became accessible post-host recovery, with customer confirmation.

title: VMWare as a Service has 2000 VMs impacted
problem: Customers were unable to connect to their virtual machines due to network connectivity issues on nsx Edge nodes
root_cause: GENEVE tunnels on the nsx Edge nodes went down because the nsx Managers were unable to communicate tunnel configuration information to the Edges. Broadcom confirmed this was caused by a known JDK issue, which was triggered during the ongoing nsx upgrade, leading to tunnel disruption
resolution: A rolling reboot of the nsx Managers restored communication with the Edge nodes and brought the GENEVE tunnels back up. As a preventive measure, Broadcom recommended performing periodic rolling reboots of the nsx Managers to avoid recurrence of this issue
impact: Customers were not able to access VMs

title: Veeam backup server issues
problem: Users were unable to start virtual machine backups as the DAL W082 Veeam Backup & Replication (VBR) server experienced an outage and sustained performance degradation
root_cause: The outage was initially caused by the PostgreSQL database process failing to start, which disrupted VBR functionality. Although a reboot temporarily restored services, the VBR server continued to perform slowly. Disk performance analysis of the VBR VM revealed high latency ratios, indicating underlying storage slowness. Further infrastructure investigation identified an ongoing large snapshot deletion (over 11 TB) on vRlog VMs, which was causing elevated vsan storage and VM disk latency. This storage contention likely contributed to degraded VBR performance and intermittent service failures.
resolution: Several vRlog VM snapshot deletions were aborted, and active vRlog backup jobs were stopped to reduce storage load. This action improved VBR responsiveness, though performance remained constrained. Subsequently, the PostgreSQL database was restarted, Uptycs tags were corrected, and all Veeam backup services were restarted. These steps restored normal VBR operations and allowed backup activities to resume successfully.
impact: Customers were not able to start backups
cbc_link: Implement Automated Snapshot Cleanup process for Mgmt VMs

title: nsx cluster status showing as UNAVAILABLE
problem: The nsx‑T Manager cluster experienced unavailability, impacting management plane operations.
root_cause: The unavailability was primarily driven by Corfu‑related symptoms, which were intensified by elevated vsan latency caused by resynchronization activity. This behavior was not observed in other environments, indicating that increased storage latency amplified known Corfu sensitivity, ultimately leading to the failure. vsan utilization had crossed recommended operational thresholds (typically ~70%), resulting in performance degradation. Similar cases previously confirmed by Broadcom have shown that storage latency can adversely impact nsx‑T stability, emphasizing the importance of strict capacity management and proactive monitoring.
resolution: A rolling reboot of the nsx‑T Managers restored cluster availability. As a preventive measure, Broadcom recommended performing periodic rolling reboots of the nsx Managers and enforcing proactive vsan capacity and latency monitoring to prevent recurrence.
